did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a multistage multiclass convolutional neural network (CNN) framework with transfer learning to classify dermoscopic images into benign and malignant skin lesions and their subcategories. The method involved developing a 26-layer CNN from scratch for initial classification and using frozen weights for subclassification, achieving 93.4% accuracy for binary classification and 96.2% for subclassification on the ISIC2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new multistage multiclass convolutional neural network framework for skin lesion detection. It details the development of a 26-layer CNN from scratch, the application of transfer learning, and the validation of results on the ISIC2018 dataset, reporting specific accuracy metrics (93.4% and 96.2%) and performance comparisons with existing models.

title: Enhancing Skin Lesion Detection: A Multistage Multiclass Convolutional Neural Network-Based Framework
brief_summary: This study proposes a multistage multiclass convolutional neural network (CNN) framework to enhance the detection and classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The method first employs a custom 26-layer CNN developed from scratch to classify lesions into benign and malignant categories, achieving 93.4% accuracy on the ISIC2018 dataset. Subsequently, transfer learning is applied to subclassify benign lesions into five categories and malignant lesions into two categories, resulting in a 96.2% accuracy rate. The framework was validated using sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score metrics, demonstrating robustness through learning curves and 10-fold cross-validation. Compared to existing pretrained models, the proposed approach achieved higher classification rates with significantly reduced training time. The results confirm the effectiveness of the multistage multiclass CNN framework for assisting in early skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant subclassification target; grouped with BCC for binary classification.
Malignant subclassification target; grouped with MEL for binary classification.
Benign subclassification target; grouped with AK, BKL, DF, and VASC for binary classification.
Benign subclassification target; grouped with NV, BKL, DF, and VASC for binary classification.
Benign subclassification target; grouped with NV, AK, DF, and VASC for binary classification.
Benign subclassification target; grouped with NV, AK, BKL, and VASC for binary classification.
Benign subclassification target; grouped with NV, AK, BKL, and DF for binary classification.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign and malignant categories, followed by multiclass subclassification of benign lesions into five subcategories (melanocytic nevus, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular) and malignant lesions into two subcategories (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a multistage multiclass convolutional neural network framework. Stage 1 uses a 26-layer CNN to classify dermoscopic images into two discrete classes: benign vs. malignant (93.4% accuracy). Stage 2 uses transfer learning with frozen weights to subclassify the benign class into five discrete categories and the malignant class into two discrete categories (96.2% accuracy). The task is explicitly defined as classification, with metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score for discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2018 (HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A public online skin lesions dataset consisting of 10,015 high-resolution dermatoscopic images collected over two decades from the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and Cliff Rosendahl’s skin cancer practice in Queensland, Australia. The dataset includes clinical diagnoses annotated by professional dermatologists.

use.training: Used for training the custom 26-layer CNN (CNN-1) for binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and for subclassification using transfer learning (CNN-2 and CNN-3). Data augmentation was applied to balance the dataset to 1000 samples per class.
use.validation: Used for validation of the models, with performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score) reported on validation sets during training and testing phases.
use.testing: Used for testing the final models to evaluate classification performance against pretrained CNNs (ResNet50, Inception V3, GoogleNet, DenseNet-201).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To further validate the performance and robustness of the proposed multistage multiclass approach (CNN-1, CNN-2, and CNN-3).

To determine the optimal architecture (number of layers) for the developed CNN by comparing performance metrics (loss, accuracy, training time) across different layer configurations (22, 26, 30, and 34 layers).
To balance the dataset by ensuring each of the seven classes had 1000 samples, thereby addressing class imbalance before training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score, and used an ablation study and 10-fold cross-validation to validate model performance and robustness.

binary_classification.notes: The dataset was balanced via augmentation to 1000 samples per class. Split ratio for training/testing was 80:20. CNN-1 outperformed pretrained models in terms of training time while maintaining competitive accuracy.

malignant_subclassification.notes: CNN-2 subclassifies malignant lesions into BCC and MEL. The text notes a true positive rate (sensitivity) of 98.5% and 94% for BCC and MEL respectively in the narrative, which aligns with the table values of 0.985 and 0.940.

benign_subclassification.notes: CNN-3 subclassifies benign lesions into AK, BKL, DF, NV, and VASC. The text highlights VASC and DF had 100% accuracy (sensitivity), while BKL had the lowest sensitivity at 87.5%.

ablation_study.notes: The 26-layer CNN was selected as it provided the best balance of validation accuracy (93.4%) and training time.

cross_validation.notes: Results from 10-fold cross-validation are reported in Table 6. The binary classification accuracy was 93.4% (Table 3) and 94.2% (Table 6 CV). The subclassification accuracy was 96.2% (Table 4) and 95.3%/97.5% (Table 6 CV).

architecture_used.main_model: Multistage Multiclass Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework consisting of three models: CNN-1 (custom 26-layer CNN for binary classification), CNN-2 (transfer-learned CNN for malignant subclassification), and CNN-3 (transfer-learned CNN for benign subclassification).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was applied using the frozen weights of the pre-trained CNN-1 model for CNN-2 and CNN-3. Pretrained models ResNet50, Inception V3, GoogleNet, and DenseNet-201 were used for comparison.

CNN-1.notes: Selected via ablation study (comparing 22, 26, 30, and 34 layers) as it provided the best balance of validation accuracy (93.4%) and training time.

CNN-3.task: Benign subclassification (AK, BKL, DF, NV, VASC)

training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy (implied by classification task and loss values)
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation and translation to balance dataset to 1000 samples per class

classification_or_output_head.CNN-3: Multiclass classification (Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic Nevus, Vascular Lesion)

preprocessing_techniques: Noise reduction using erosion and dilatation techniques
Cropping to remove undesirable regions and estimate extreme points
Data augmentation using rotation and translation to balance the dataset (1000 samples per class)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance the dataset, ensuring each of the seven classes had 1000 samples.

conclusions: The proposed multistage multiclass convolutional neural network (CNN) framework effectively enhances skin lesion detection by achieving a binary classification accuracy of 93.4% for distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions on the ISIC2018 dataset.

The framework achieved a high subclassification accuracy of 96.2% for both benign (five subcategories) and malignant (two subcategories) lesions using transfer learning with frozen weights from the initial binary classification model.

The custom 26-layer CNN developed for binary classification outperformed existing pretrained models (ResNet50, Inception V3, GoogleNet, DenseNet-201) in terms of training efficiency, requiring significantly less training time (11 min 41 s vs. 67 min to 1227 min) while maintaining competitive or superior classification rates.
The ablation study confirmed that the 26-layer architecture provided the optimal balance between validation accuracy (93.4%) and training time compared to 22, 30, and 34-layer configurations.

The multistage approach demonstrated robustness through 10-fold cross-validation, yielding accuracies of 94.2% for binary classification, 97.5% for malignant subclassification, and 95.3% for benign subclassification.
Specific subclassification performance varied, with vascular lesions (VASC) and dermatofibromas (DF) achieving 100% sensitivity, while benign keratosis (BKL) had the lowest sensitivity at 87.5%.
The study concludes that the proposed framework offers a viable, computationally efficient alternative to complex pretrained models for assisting in early skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC2018 (HAM10000) dataset, which introduces potential population bias as the data was collected from only two specific locations (Vienna, Austria and Queensland, Australia) over two decades.

The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (e.g., 6705 NV vs. 115 DF samples), which was addressed by aggressive data augmentation to balance classes to 1000 samples each; this may artificially inflate performance metrics and reduce generalizability to real-world distributions.
No external validation was performed; the model was only validated internally using an 80:20 split and 10-fold cross-validation on the single source dataset.
CNN hyperparameters were not fine-tuned, which limits the potential performance of the proposed architecture and suggests the results may not represent the model's upper bound.
The custom 26-layer CNN achieved 100% training accuracy with very low validation loss, raising potential concerns about overfitting despite the use of augmentation and cross-validation.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to compare the proposed method against baseline models, relying instead on point estimates of accuracy and metrics.

The subclassification performance varied significantly, with Benign Keratosis (BKL) showing the lowest sensitivity (87.5%), indicating potential weakness in distinguishing this specific benign lesion type.

The study claims computational efficiency (11 min 41 s training time) compared to pretrained models, but does not provide inference latency or memory footprint metrics, which are critical for clinical deployment considerations.
